Space road trip — four destinations, reordered

Per Ben's explicit direction: no aurora, no firefly meadow — back to four stops, reordered so Midnight Galaxy opens the tour, then Space Battle (rebuilt again since from a meteor shower, see the dated notes below), then the planet cluster — weaving between a small group of differently colored planets, banking around each in turn — then Autumn Harvest / Supernova to close. This file went 4 stops → 6 stops (added northern lights + firefly meadow, both audited and fixed) → back to 4 on Ben's call after a third-party review raised pacing questions about the 6-stop order — the aurora/firefly worlds and their fixes are removed outright, not just hidden, since they're not part of the approved sequence. Important: in the real app these still fire as four separate round-boundary journeys, each at its own round transition — this combined file is a review artifact for watching the whole arc at once, not how it plays live.
stop 1 / 4 — midnight galaxy
phase: idle
Structure — a generalized N-stop tent-function crossfade, unchanged in principle: this file tracks one continuous tourProgress value (now 0=galaxy, 1=meteor, 2=gas station, 3=harvest) that ramps between integers during each bridge window and holds flat during each stop's hold phase; every world's opacity is clamp01(1 - |tourProgress - itsIndex|). TOUR_KF is auto-derived from TIMELINE via buildTourKeyframes() (added during the accessibility/architecture review pass, kept here) — reordering and dropping stops just meant editing TIMELINE's phase list; the keyframes rebuilt themselves correctly with no hand-editing of offsets.

Sequence (~43.6s total, four stops — three still ~10s per stop, the floating-diner stop extended to ~13.6s 2026-07-22, see below): midnight-galaxy hold (10,000ms, opens the tour — z-depth star warp ramping up from rest + nebula wash, no bridge before it since it's first) → bridge to meteor shower (600ms) → meteor-shower hold (2900ms) + departure streaks (6500ms, its own established two-phase hold-then-flourish pattern, ratio kept proportional to the original 400:900 split) → bridge to the floating diner (600ms) → floating-diner hold (13,000ms: a small floating rock with a diner on it, a dominant ringed planet behind, ship flies in slow and parks beside the diner, idle beat, a small roof-launched drone delivers takeout, DINER sign roof-mounted on support posts, clustered pixel lights, neon buzzes in then holds steady) → bridge to harvest (600ms) → a plain ambient hold (7000ms, ember field only, no convergence yet) → harvest converge + supernova burst + settle (2400ms, timing untouched from the already-audited version). Three of the four stops still sum to almost exactly 10,000ms including their own bridge-in; the floating-diner stop is the one deliberate exception this pass, still within round-journeys.md's 8-14s per-journey range once split out as its own real round-boundary journey.

What changed this pass: northern lights and firefly meadow — both built, both audited, both fixed in two separate review passes — are removed outright per Ben's explicit direction ("no aurora... midnight galaxy, then meteor shower, then gas stop, then supernova"), not just hidden behind a flag. Their worlds, particle arrays, palette objects, and HUD branches are deleted, not commented out — a review that flagged this exact file for maintainability risk from having too many worlds in one place; leaving dead code in after removing them from the tour would have been the same smell in a different shape. Midnight galaxy moves from the second stop to the very first — it now owns the "opening" hold-and-warp beat that meteor shower used to open with; meteor shower keeps its own established hold-then-streaks pattern, just fires as the second stop instead of the first.

Reused vs. new, four-stop version: galaxy star-warp + nebula (from midnight-galaxy-spaceship-journey.html), meteor star/streak field (from the round-boundary file), harvest converge/burst (from galaxy-to-harvest-handoff-journey.html) — all reused verbatim. Gas station (ground, sign, canopy, pumps, clustered pixel lights, one-shot neon buzz-in) is the one genuinely new world, carried over unchanged from the six-stop version along with its own two audit passes' fixes.

Real theme colors, all four: midnight-galaxy (bg #08001a, accent #4a1a8f, highlight #c060ff), meteor-shower (bg #020408, highlight #e0f0ff), retro-arcade (bg #040010, accent #3a0880, highlight #a020ff, shinyAccent #20ff80), autumn-harvest (bg #1a0800, accent #7a2808, highlight #ff6820) — all pulled directly from their real entries in themes/index.js.

Re-keyed for the new order (verified, not assumed): the gas station's power-on now starts 300ms into bridge2 (meteor→gas), not bridge3 — bridge2 is gas's actual incoming bridge in the new order. galCoreFade (the extra fade on galaxy's bright core gradient during its own outgoing bridge) is now keyed to index 0 (1-clamp01(tp*2)), since galaxy is index 0 now, not 1. The galaxy star-warp ramp-up now fires during galHold alone (it's the opening phase, so there's no incoming bridge to also gate on) and decays during everything after — previously it ramped during an incoming bridge plus its hold, which doesn't apply to an opening stop.

Carried over from the last two review passes (still true, not re-litigated here): supernova flash widened to a ~440ms attack capped at 0.32 peak alpha (was a ~240ms spike to 0.6 — an eye-strain concern for a dark-venue live show); HUD has aria-live="polite"; reduced motion keeps opacity-only twinkle/blink breathing everywhere (galaxy stars, gas station pixel lights) rather than freezing to a flat value, while disabling actual translation; gas station ground/sign/canopy/lettering contrast and clustering fixes; captions use each palette's textHex, gated on real state (harvest's on novaFlash being low) rather than fixed elapsed-time guesses.

Known rough edges, not hidden: the gas-station sign/canopy/pumps and moon-ground silhouette are hand-drawn canvas primitives, not generated assets. The tent-function crossfade is a straight linear ramp between keyframes, not hand-tuned per bridge — a specific pair might want a longer or shorter bridge than the fixed 600ms used everywhere here. The floating-diner stop's arrival beat is now a lightweight camera-POV push (scale/translate on drawGasWorld's own content, no cockpit-UI chrome) — see the 2026-07-22 camera-POV notes paragraph below; the other three stops (galaxy, meteor, harvest) remain pure ambient-world transformation with no camera-POV move of their own, and the full dashboard-overlay treatment (struts/dash/vignette/pilot seat) still lives only in the separate liftoff file, not here. A production-readiness critique from the third review (refactor each world into class-based init()/update()/render()/destroy() objects) is good input for the eventual real-app build, not applied here — this file doesn't ship as-is.

Reduced motion: all motion (streaks, warp, sign flicker, pixel blinking, converge/burst) is disabled or reduced to opacity-only breathing; each world crossfades in as a calm field at the same relative timing the full version uses, and the caption/stop-index text still updates so all four destinations are still legible without the animation.

Second Fable pass, after the 10s-per-stop runtime stretch — five findings, all fixed: (1) CRITICAL — harAmbient's 12 embers spawned entirely below-frame (y:H+rand*H*0.3) and rose too slowly to reliably enter frame within the new 7000ms harHold, reading as a near-empty screen; fixed by spawning across the full frame height, bumping count 12→32 and alpha 0.18→0.3, and reseeding on every resetUI() replay. (2) CRITICAL, and mine to own — when buildTourKeyframes() replaced the old hand-written TOUR_KF, it only pushed a flat keyframe for phases ending in 'Hold' or equal to harConverge, silently missing msOut; invisible at msOut's old ~900ms length, but a real ~7100ms visible bug once msOut stretched to 6500ms — the tour would drift toward gas station for the whole departure flourish instead of holding at meteor shower until the bridge. Fixed by making every non-bridge phase push its own flat endpoint, the actually-correct general rule. (3) gas station's neon sign held a dead-flat 0.95 for ~93% of its new 9400ms hold; added a slow idle wobble plus one scheduled stutter-dip around the 5s mark. (4) galaxy's background warp ramped to a flat 0.42 target and sat there monotone for ~8.7s of its 10s hold; swapped for a slow breathing swell. (5) the letterbox fade-in scaled off GAL_HOLD*0.6, which read fine at the old short hold but stretched to a visible 6000ms fade at the new 10s GAL_HOLD; fixed to a flat 700ms regardless of hold length. All five verified via node --check plus a re-run of the exact-timing trace confirming the 40,000ms total and each stop's 10,000ms budget were unaffected by any of these visual fixes.

External (Gemini) review, this pass — two real fixes applied, one number independently corrected, several flagged as opinion/future-work rather than applied: (1) caught that drawGasWorld's reduced-motion path hardcoded the neon sign to a flat 0.95, bypassing neonBuzz() and, with it, the file's own established convention of keeping opacity-only breathing alive under reduced motion (galaxy stars, pixel lights both already did this) — fixed with a slow 1500ms sine breathing between 0.70-0.95, no translation, no steep flicker. (2) flagged the moon-horizon silhouette (#14082a on ARC.bg) as low-contrast and proposed #1c0d38 as a fix; independently recomputing WCAG relative-luminance contrast for that exact pair gives ~1.15:1 — statistically the same as the ~1.08:1 original, not a real fix — so #54329a is used instead, verified at ~2.27:1. Not applied, flagged as Ben's call: the review's opinion that the new 10s Midnight Galaxy hold "feels bloated" for an opening beat, and that Autumn Harvest's 7s calm-ember lead-in risks feeling abrupt going into its 700ms converge — both are pacing/feel judgments about the explicit 10s-per-stop direction, not bugs, so they're surfaced here rather than silently acted on. Not applied, correctly scoped as future-app work (same as the prior review's OOP suggestion): encapsulating each world's particle state into factory/class objects and moving static canvas paths to Path2D — good input for the real build, out of scope for this single-file prototype.

Rework pass, Ben's direct call after watching it — "the gas station scene looks bad," "the meteor shower didn't feel like a shower at all... we're supposed to be going THROUGH the meteor shower," "the spaceship is supposed to shake as we go through," plus a diner-on-Mars reference image and "this is a story, a visual story": Meteor shower was rebuilt from a calm corner-radiant-plus-occasional-streak (how you'd watch a shower from a hillside) into an actual z-depth debris field you fly through — msDebris reuses the same project-and-pull-toward-camera trick drawGalWorld's star warp already used in this file, but with chunkier rock/ice fragments and real motion-blur trails, at an intensity that ramps 0→1 over the first 2.5s of msHold (entering the shower) and stays at 1 through msOut (in it, then departing) — the stop now has a felt beginning and middle, not just a hold plus a tacked-on flourish. A camera shake (msShake, the same time-based dual-sine noise technique as the liftoff POV file's engine rumble, so it's robust to dropped frames) is applied to the canvas element itself — not the world draw — so the letterbox and HUD stay steady while the view through them jostles, reading as being inside a shaken cockpit. Checked and confirmed: the Kirupa z-depth-starfield technique referenced earlier this session had already been applied to the liftoff POV file's warp field, but never made it into this file's meteor world — that gap is exactly why it read flat. Gas station got a real focal point it never had: a ringed planet dominating the sky (ring drawn behind, planet body, then a bright ring arc in front, for cheap depth) plus two layers of jagged mesa/butte silhouettes (far, dim; near, bold) replacing the single smooth horizon bump — both in this theme's own real palette, not the reference photo's literal colors. The far mesa layer's first color (rgba(60,32,110,0.55)) composited to only ~1.23:1 against ARC.bg — caught and independently re-verified before shipping it, same discipline as every contrast check elsewhere in this file — and replaced with rgba(100,56,180,0.8), which composites to ~2.12:1. Both reworks keep every existing reduced-motion contract: meteor debris drops to opacity-only presence with no z-rush and no trail, camera shake is fully disabled, and the new planet/mesas are static shapes with no motion to begin with. Separately confirmed against the real production code (not guessed): during an actual live round, meteor-shower's ambient backdrop (ParticleBackground.jsx) is intentionally calm — a passive starfield, a milky-way wash, and one lone streak every 1.5-4s, built not to compete with trivia content on screen — and retro-arcade isn't a bespoke scene at all during a round, it's routed through a generic "electric" breathing gradient with no gas-station art in production. That's by design: the round JOURNEY (what these prototype files are) is supposed to be the one moment of real motion and spectacle, distinctly more kinetic than the calm backdrop a round itself sits on — so pushing this hard on "feels like flying through it" is the correct instinct, not overkill relative to how the app actually behaves during play.

Cross-session finding, then a second rework of the gas station: a parallel Cowork session ("alongside") is building the actual production framework for generating round-journey concepts for other themes going forward, and its plan is built directly on top of this repo's own references/round-journeys.md and references/ambient-design-law.md — both already in the repo, neither read before tonight's original prototyping pass. Their rule: a journey is "built on top of something the theme or app already owns... never a parallel system invented from scratch," checked via the real ParticleBackground.jsx BEFORE design starts, not after. Retested against that rule: retro-arcade has no bespoke in-round ambient to extend (it's a generic gradient in production), so inventing a new world for it is the correct move, matching the framework's own "empty slate → invent fresh" allowance. Meteor shower's debris-field rework, by contrast, is a genuine parallel invention rather than an extension of the real MeteorShowerStreak motif — flagged to Ben, not yet resolved, since "flying through it" and "extend the one-streak-at-a-time motif" are in real tension and it's his call which wins.

Gas station, second rework — Ben's direct call: "we need to park outside the diner," then "another ship, a small diner. go": the whole stop was a static held frame (a sign flickering on, nothing else ever happening) — exactly the failure mode round-journeys.md's own five-part scene checklist names outright ("what is it *doing*? avoid a static held pose as the only content"). Fixed with a real arrival beat: a small ship (drawGasShip/drawGasShipBody, its own dark silhouette + rim-light, same convention as every other sprite built tonight) flies in from off-frame after a 300ms anticipation hold, travels a shallow arc (not a straight diagonal — motion-follows-arcs from the technique checklist) with an eased fast-start/smooth-settle curve, and lands with a brief engine-flare that fades a beat after the hull itself stops (follow-through, not a simultaneous hard cut), then idles at a parked position beside the diner with a slow low-amplitude bob and idle engine-glow breathing for the rest of the hold. The sign itself was relabeled "GAS"→"DINER" and shrunk ("a small diner," not a billboard), and the old canopy-plus-two-pumps read (a fuel stop) was replaced with a small storefront wall and a warm interior window-glow band in ARC.hi (in-family, not an arbitrary warm hue). Reduced motion: the ship appears already parked with no flight-in and no bob-translate, only opacity-only engine-glow breathing — same contract as every other moving element in this file.

Third Fable pass, on the ship/diner rework and the meteor debris field — 10 findings, all fixed: (1) HIGH — meteor shower's reduced-motion branch was false advertising: base stayed 0.006 even when reduced and the z-decrement ran unconditionally, so debris still visibly rushed (full depth traversal in ~2.7s) despite the comments and notes claiming "no z-rush." Reduced now genuinely freezes each fragment's z (no decrement, no reset) and only adds a slow opacity twinkle. (2) HIGH — the ship's touchdown flare could be *set* up to ~650ms before it was actually *rendered*, because the old code branched on wall-clock t >= SHIP_FLIGHT_DUR while the eased position had already gone visually static much earlier — the flash read as disconnected from the landing. Rewritten as one continuous position formula for all t (no seam) with a settle value derived from easeOutCubic's own remaining-distance curve (independently verified: remaining travel is under 0.6px by the time settle starts ramping) — the flare and idle-bob now key off actual near-zero motion, not a guessed timestamp. (3) touchdown-flare decay wasn't dtn-normalized (the one decay in the file that wasn't) — fixed. (4) the parked ship's hull directly overlapped the middle near-mesa, and the ship's rim-light — its whole legibility device — computed to only ~1.2:1 against that mesa's purple exactly where it parks; moved the mesa left, independently verified at a clean 48px gap. (5) rock debris chunks were drawn under the same 'lighter' composite as everything else in that loop, so their dark fill could only add faint luminance and never actually occlude — they rendered as bare rings, not chunks; switched just that fill to source-over. (6) debris speed hard-cut ~9x the instant msOut ends even though the meteor world is still ~94% opaque at that boundary — added an exponentially-smoothed intensity (msIntensitySmooth) so both the debris speed and the shake amplitude ease across that seam instead of stepping. (7) the diner's wall-edge rects used a fixed height that left a ~6px gap of visible sky under the whole building, currently hidden only by dark-on-dark color coincidence; walls now run to the actual ground line at that x. (8) shake amplitude's flat +1.4px floor popped in the instant msHold started; now scales purely off the smoothed intensity, so it fades in with everything else. (9) removed a leftover fixed corner-radiant glow from the pre-rework "watch it from a hillside" design — an orphaned sourceless bright patch the debris field now rushes across for no reason. (10) narrowed the clustered pixel-lights' spread (±120→±70) after confirming the wider spread's max x landed inside the ringed planet's disc, rendering as sparkle artifacts on the planet face instead of station lights. All fixed and independently re-verified via node --check plus hand-recomputed pixel geometry (hull/mesa gap, remaining-distance-at-settle) and timing sums — 40,000ms total and each stop's 10,000ms budget unaffected. Fable's separate opinion (not a bug, logged for the record): the gas-station frame's density — planet, two mesa layers, ship, sign/storefront, pixel lights — is appropriate for a 9.4s "we've arrived" beat now that the two real overlap points (ship/mesa, lights/planet) are fixed; nothing was flagged as needing removal.

"Make them all MEMORABLE" pass: a fresh-eyes guest-reaction simulation (a subagent reading the code cold and roleplaying a first-time Baynes trivia guest, evaluating each stop as its own isolated moment spread across a real night, never seeing the dev notes) came back with an honest split: meteor shower's shake and harvest's supernova burst genuinely landed ("made the whole table look up"), the gas station's ship-landing was liked but read as "cute, not epic," and midnight galaxy was the real miss — "wallpaper, not a highlight," no actual event, would go unnoticed. The common thread across the two that worked: each has exactly one unmistakable, single-instant event, not just prettier ambiance. Two additions, both Ben's explicit calls: (1) Galaxy hyperspace snap — considered and rejected a comet/shooting-star idea on Ben's own instinct that it would compete with meteor shower's whole identity (a bright thing streaking through dark space); a comet would have been a new object doing the same basic trick. Instead GAL_SNAP_AT (4200ms into galHold) triggers galSnapEnvelope(), a fast-attack/slow-decay pulse that spikes the EXISTING galWarp value hard — no new particle system, just the star-warp math this file already has, pushed to an extreme for one beat, stretching the calm stars into fast streaks — plus a capped white flash (0.32 peak alpha, same eye-comfort discipline as the supernova) and a brief canvas zoom-punch (scale(), not translate — a different motion grammar from meteor's jitter so the two stops don't feel like variations of the same trick). Independently confirmed against the real MidnightGalaxyAmbient production component: the actual in-round backdrop has zero motion beyond opacity-only twinkle, so this warp mechanic was already 100% journey-only invention with nothing to clash with. (2) Gas station touchdown thump — Ben's pick over leaving it as-is: a one-shot camera jolt (shipThump, reusing msRumbleNoise at a much smaller amplitude and a ~150-200ms decay — an order of magnitude shorter than meteor's sustained rumble) plus a new ground-dust puff (dustPuff, expanding at the ship's actual ground-contact point) fire together, edge-triggered exactly once via shipLanded, at the same instant the existing engine flare begins. Both additions respect every existing reduced-motion contract: galSnapEnv and the gas jolt are both force-zeroed under reduced motion, so neither stop gains any new motion in that mode — verified via node --check and a re-run of the timing trace confirming 40,000ms total / 10,000ms-per-stop untouched.

v2 — nightly agent revision pass (2026-07-20), fixing Ben's two morning-review notes on the v1 file, nothing else touched: (1) Missing visibilitychange handler — tStart only reset on the on-page Replay click; backgrounding the tab and returning mid-sequence would make tick()'s total = now - tStart jump forward by the whole hidden duration in one frame, skipping or snapping ahead unpredictably. Fixed exactly as specified: a visibilitychange listener now stops the loop while hidden and calls the same full reset the Replay button already uses when the tab returns to visible — no new reset mechanism, the existing one reused. (2) Missing the postMessage contract — this file had a working Replay button and reduced-motion toggle but no window.__journeyControls, no embedded postmessage-child-boilerplate.js, and no pause at all. The boilerplate is now embedded verbatim in its own <script> tag. replay is the renamed former play() function (full reset — same behavior, new name only, to free up play for the contract's distinct resume verb) and is the exact same function the on-page button calls, so there is one replay implementation, not two. pause is a real implementation, not a stub: it cancels the pending animation frame so nothing advances or redraws while paused, and records the pause timestamp. play (the contract's resume verb, distinct from the internal replay) shifts tStart forward by exactly the paused duration before resuming, so the sequence continues from the same elapsed position instead of jumping — the same underlying fix pattern as the visibilitychange handler, applied to an explicit pause instead of a backgrounded tab. Nothing else in this file changed: no sprite, palette, timing constant, or draw call from the file this superseded was touched.

v2 audit scope note (Step 5, honest scope): this revision pass only touched the shared-state/control-flow block at the bottom of the script and added one new embedded script tag; every world's draw code, palette, and timing constant above is byte-identical to the superseded file. The 40,000ms combined-file runtime and each stop's individual ~10,000ms budget are unaffected by this pass — this file's own already-documented exception to the normal 8-14s single-journey duration target (a deliberate four-stop combined review artifact, not a single round-boundary journey; see the brief in QUEUE.md) is unchanged and was not re-litigated here, per the instruction to fix exactly what Ben named and nothing else.

v2 Step 5 audit results (code-invariant checks only — no browser in this runtime, so runtime feel/actual TV banding/motion-smoothing remain explicitly Ben's job, same as every prior pass): contrast — no text/background pairing changed by this pass, prior passes' verified ratios stand. Timing sums — total independently re-added from the eleven TIMELINE segment durations: 40,000ms, matches the notes above; unaffected since no duration constant was touched. Frame-rate independence — dtn normalization present and untouched; the clock reset is now wired to both replay() and visibilitychange, which is the fix itself. GPU-only compliance — grepped the full file for animated width/height/color/box-shadow/filter: one pre-existing hit, hud.style.color=hudColor in tick(), assigns a discrete per-phase value (not a continuous interpolation) redundantly every frame within a phase — a minor wasted-recalc smell, not a visual GPU-only violation, present unchanged since before this file's three Fable and two Gemini review passes and never flagged by any of them; noted here rather than silently passed over, left as-is since it's neither of Ben's two revision notes and this pass's own scope is fix-exactly-what-was-named. Silhouette legibility, particle pooling (msDebris/galStars/pixelLights/harAmbient all confirmed still fixed-size, still reused not reallocated), and near-black banding mitigation — all unchanged, none of this pass's edits touch drawing code. No external references — grepped for http:///https:///img.recraft.ai: none found. Sanitizer-passed SVG only — not applicable, this file has zero Recraft-generated sprites (hand-drawn canvas primitives throughout, per its own long-standing notes); the one static <svg> is the hand-authored grain-texture filter, never sanitizer-scoped. postMessage contract present — confirmed byte-for-byte identical to concepts/tools/postmessage-child-boilerplate.js (diffed programmatically, not eyeballed), __journeyControls wired to real play/pause/replay implementations, the on-page button and the contract call the exact same replay() function. Reduced-motion branch — read, not run: unchanged by this pass, still correctly force-zeroes galSnapEnv/shake/ship-flight/z-decrement under reduced. Both new <script> blocks additionally passed node --check for syntax validity, and a full-file diff against the superseded file confirmed the change is confined to exactly the notes paragraph, one new state variable, and the control-flow block at the script's tail — nothing in any world's draw code, palette, or timing constant was touched.

Known open item, carried forward unresolved (Ben's call, not this pass's to make): whether the meteor-shower z-depth debris-field rework is a parallel invention vs. an extension of the real MeteorShowerStreak motif in ParticleBackground.jsx, exactly as flagged in QUEUE.md — not touched by this revision pass, which only addressed the two named technical notes.

Floating-diner redesign (2026-07-22), from a live grilling session, not a vague note: Ben's own words on the prior version — "the space diner sucks... it doesn't pull anything into it that I want... it's supposed to be a distant planet that happens to have a diner" — plus a Minecraft screenshot (a small structure perched on a floating rock, planet looming behind) as concrete reference. The full grilled direction, locked in QUEUE.md's revision notes before this build pass started, and implemented here beat for beat: composition — the moon-surface ground (full-width, standing-on-a-surface) is replaced with drawFloatingIsland(), a bounded rock shape (ISLAND_LEFT/ISLAND_RIGHT, W*0.24-0.80) that leaves visible starfield below and beside it, so the read is "floating," not "standing"; the far/near mesa arrays are repurposed from horizon-attached buttes into independent floating debris-rocks (drawGasMesa now takes a y01 and draws a shape tapered top AND bottom); the dominant ringed planet is untouched and unmoved — leaned into by removing everything that used to compete with it for "the thing you look at" (a full horizon skyline), not by making it bigger. Ground debris, not dust — the old soft dustPuff radial-gradient blob is replaced with gasDebrisBits, a fixed 10-particle pool (pre-allocated, reset not reallocated on each trigger and each replay, same discipline as every other particle field in this file) that kicks upward-and-outward from the ship's real ground-contact point on touchdown, three of the ten using the new pink accent. Palette — stays purple-dominant; every spot that used to reach for ARC.shiny (green) within this one world (sign glow/lettering, planet ring, pixel lights, ship engine/flare) now uses a new FLYBY_TEAL, and a new DINER_PINK is introduced but reserved ONLY for the two new one-shot moments (debris spark, drone delivery blip) — an accent shift, not a repaint, per the composition checklist's 60/30/10 guidance. Blocking — the ship still parks BESIDE the diner, not into it (this file never had a push-in camera to begin with — tick()'s only camera transforms are the meteor shake and the galaxy zoom-punch, neither touches this stop — so "hold wide, don't pull in" required no removal, only confirming it was never there). Flight-in slowed — SHIP_HOLD_BEFORE 300→500, SHIP_FLIGHT_DUR 1700→2800; both the arc and the eased-settle math are fractions of these constants, so slowing them down needed no other changes. Idle beat, then payoff — a new derived timeline (SHIP_TOUCHDOWN_AT through DRONE_DONE_AT) inserts an explicit 1800ms idle hold after touchdown before drawDinerDrone() launches a small on-palette drone from a roof dock, down to the parked ship, one delivery-blip pulse (the pink accent's one use), then back up to dock — "a takeout order be brought to us," "mount it to the roof," per Ben's direct call. Signage — the DINER sign moved from a single tall ground-rooted pole to two short posts planted directly in the roofline (canopyY), per "roof-mounted with visible support posts"; the building itself is now drawn BEFORE the sign so the posts visually plant into an already-drawn roof rather than passing through an undrawn one. Interior — unchanged; the window-glow band was already minimal (a lit band, not rendered contents) and stays that way per Ben's "keep it a silhouette/glow read." Duration — GAS_HOLD extended 9400→13000ms to fit the slower flight, idle beat, and drone beat without rushing any of them (file TOTAL is now 43,600ms, up from 40,000ms — only this one stop's duration changed; still within round-journeys.md's 8-14s per-journey range once split apart). Every new animated element (debris, drone) is fully absent or reduced to a static docked/opacity-only state under reduced, matching every existing element's contract in this file — verified via node --check and by hand-tracing the derived timeline constants (see this pass's own STEP 5 audit results, next).

Step 5 visual pass, this same 2026-07-22 build — one real finding, fixed within the one-attempt rule: ran visual-audit.mjs against this file (scoped to a 34s window covering the whole floating-diner stop plus surrounding context — this stop's own generic phase-label doesn't distinguish the new touchdown/debris/drone sub-beats from the rest of gasHold, so a supplementary targeted spot-check script hit 8 exact timestamps inside those sub-beats directly, rather than trusting only the generic sampler's phase-change-driven coverage — see concepts/tools/spot-check.mjs, added this pass as a reusable companion for future single-phase sub-beat verification). Actually looking at the rendered frames (not just reading the draw code) surfaced one real problem the code review missed: the repurposed far/near "debris rock" layer (drawGasMesa's first pass this build) kept the OLD mesa function's tall, symmetric double-trapezoid shape and size — rendered, it read as a row of giant purple VASES/PILLARS flanking the island, not small floating debris, undermining the whole "floating in the void" composition goal. Fixed, the one allowed attempt: reworked drawGasMesa into a genuinely small, irregular 7-point jagged blob (deterministically jittered from each planet's own x01, stable across replays with no new persistent state), echoing msDebris's own rock-chunk visual language from the meteor-shower world earlier in the tour. Re-checked once via the same spot-check script: the composition now reads as intended — small scattered rock debris around a clearly bounded floating island, planet still the dominant background element, ship/sign/drone all legible against it. Secondary finding in the same look: the new drone (drawDroneBody) was hard to resolve at its original W*0.028 width — read as a fuzzy dot, arms/rotors too small to see. Bumped to W*0.045 (still roughly half the ship's own scale) in the same fix pass; re-check confirmed it now reads as a small quadcopter shape with its delivery blip clearly visible. Per the one-attempt rule, no further self-fix looping past this single re-check — proceeding to the Fable second-opinion pass with both evidence bundles (the 34s scoped generic bundle and the 8-frame targeted spot-check, post-fix) referenced below.

Fable second-opinion pass (evidence-only, per the one-Fable-pass rule), this same 2026-07-22 build: confirmed all 8 checked claims against the actual screenshots (zoomed/brightened crops, not just the raw frames): floating island reads as genuinely bounded with starfield around/below it; the reworked debris rocks read as small scattered shapes, not pillars; the ringed planet stays the clear background anchor; the ship parks beside the diner with a static wide hold, no push-in; the DINER sign reads as roof-mounted on two posts, no ground pole; the drone reads as a proper small quadcopter shape with an unambiguous pink delivery blip during its hover. Two things flagged, both real, neither blocking: (1) the touchdown debris burst is genuinely present (confirmed via a 3.5x-brightened crop — a mix of pink and rock-gray fragments at the ship's contact point) but subtle at normal viewing brightness, "blink-and-miss" in Fable's words — a polish nit for a future pass, not a functional gap. (2) my own targeted spot-check script's t021300 frame (labeled "ship-mid-flight") shows no ship at all — Fable correctly traced this to the ship's flight starting fully off-frame by design (SHIP_START.x = -W*0.18, unchanged from the pre-redesign ship-arrival mechanic, only its timing was slowed this pass) rather than a real animation bug: the ship is confirmed airborne and visible by the next spot-check frame (t022600, geShip~1800ms into the 2800ms flight). The mislabel is in my verification script's own assumption about when "mid-flight" would be visible, not in the built file. Verdict: ready for Ben. Runtime feel, actual venue-TV read, and whether the debris burst reads clearly enough on a real screen (vs. this dev-monitor screenshot check) remain explicitly Ben's own morning-review job, same as every prior pass in this file.

Camera-POV rework (v4, 2026-07-22), Ben's direct call after watching v3 build: "i know this is still concept based... but remember, we are in the spaceship. i dont want to see the exterior when we go to the diner." Resolved via three follow-up questions Ben answered directly: (1) weight of the fix — "in the middle... the galaxy and meteor shower already look like we're in the cockpit" — meaning the same lightweight technique class those two stops already use (pure camera motion, zero cockpit-UI chrome), not the liftoff file's full dashboard overlay, and not a bare removal with nothing replacing it; (2) scope — diner stop only, galaxy/meteor/harvest confirmed fine as-is, none of their code touched this pass; (3) the takeout-drone beat — "leep the drone concept. the drone is briunging us dinner! lol" — kept exactly, only its delivery target moved. What changed: drawGasShip/drawGasShipBody are deleted outright (not just uncalled) — no external ship silhouette is ever drawn. The flight-in is now updateGasArrival() driving a camera push: drawGasWorld wraps everything from the ringed planet through the drone (but not the flat background fill or the deep starfield, both kept at identity so there's no edge-gap when scaled out and the far stars read as a fixed distant backdrop) in a ctx.save()/scale/translate centered on the diner itself (camFocusX/camFocusY), scaling 0.88→1 over the same SHIP_HOLD_BEFORE/SHIP_FLIGHT_DUR/easeOutCubic timing the old ship-position code used, so the felt pacing is identical even though nothing flies across frame anymore. Touchdown is a camera jolt — shipThump is untouched, it was already wired to canvas.style.transform in tick() and was never actually tied to the ship sprite. The touchdown flare and debris burst (triggerGasDebris/drawGasDebris, unchanged) are now drawn in screen space after ctx.restore(), keyed to the fixed SHIP_PARK point instead of a moving sprite position — reads as a flash across our own view rather than a glow on a hull. A new ambient bottom-edge glow (gasIdleGlow, a soft FLYBY_TEAL linear gradient) replaces the old idling-ship engine-glow-on-a-hull once landed, suggesting our own engine light spilling into view from just off-frame rather than an external idle animation. DRONE_SHIP_HOVER moved from beside SHIP_PARK to low-center-of-frame (CX, near MOON_Y) so the delivery reads as arriving at us/the camera, not at a parked external object — drawDinerDrone's own logic is otherwise untouched. SHIP_START is deleted (no sprite position to interpolate from anymore). Verified via node --check; full account of the visual pass and Fable's evidence-check for this iteration follow in the next two entries.

Step 5 visual pass, iteration 4 (camera-POV rework), 2026-07-22 — one real finding, fixed within the one-attempt rule: ran visual-audit.mjs (34s scoped window, same as the prior pass) plus concepts/tools/spot-check.mjs retargeted to this file at the same 8 sub-beat timestamps used for v3 (touchdown, debris, drone launch/hover/return/dock — still one shared gasHold phase label, still not distinguishable by the generic sampler alone). No ship/hull sprite in any frame across the whole stop, confirmed by eye — the fix's core requirement. One real bug found by actually looking at the touchdown frame: the touchdown flare's radial-gradient center was camFocusX/camFocusY (the camera's focus point, near roof height) — a copy-paste leftover from the camera-transform code just above it — while triggerGasDebris fires at the correct ground-contact point (SHIP_PARK.x, MOON_Y), so the flare rendered near the sign/roof, disconnected from the debris burst at ground level. Fixed, the one allowed attempt: re-anchored the flare's gradient center to the same SHIP_PARK.x, MOON_Y+H*0.03 point the debris uses. Re-checked once via the same spot-check script: the re-rendered frame shows the glow and debris both near ground level, no longer split. Per the one-attempt rule, no further self-fix looping past this single re-check — proceeding to the Fable pass with both the pre-fix and post-fix touchdown frames referenced below.

Fable second-opinion pass (evidence-only, per the one-Fable-pass rule), iteration 4, 2026-07-22: confirmed, reading the actual frames: no ship/hull sprite anywhere in the diner stop; no cockpit-UI chrome (no struts/dashboard/vignette/seat) — correctly "in the middle," not the liftoff file's full overlay; the arrival reads as camera motion (measured the DINER sign's own pixel bounding box growing smoothly ~0.94→1.0 scale across the push, not an object crossing frame); the drone delivery reads as arriving low-center-of-frame near the diner with a clear pink blip, no odd clipping. One finding NOT confirmed as fixed: pixel-diffing the post-fix touchdown frame against the settled-idle frame, Fable found only the debris dots — no distinct flare glow at that exact captured millisecond — where my own by-eye read of the same frame saw a soft glow alongside the debris. This is very plausibly a decay-timing artifact rather than a real positioning bug: shipTouchdownFlare decays fast (~0.06/frame, roughly a quarter-second full fade) and the spot-check's real-elapsed capture landed ~35-40ms past the exact touchdown instant, which is enough for the flare to have faded substantially by the time the shutter fired — the same class of "technically present, easy to miss in one frame" issue this file's own notes already flagged once before for the debris burst itself. Not re-attempted as a further fix this pass, per the one-attempt rule (already used on this same finding's positioning bug) — flagged here plainly rather than silently claimed clean. Also flagged by Fable, both minor and neither blocking: the ground-contact point sits noticeably left of the diner building itself (inherited unchanged from SHIP_PARK's original position, not something this pass moved), and the docked drone's sprite slightly overhangs the awning's left edge at rest (present before this pass too, not introduced by it). Whether the touchdown flare reads clearly enough at real venue-TV brightness, and the overall camera-push feel, remain explicitly Ben's own review — same as every prior pass in this file.

Bank/tilt + punch-out (v5, 2026-07-22), Ben's direct call after watching v4: "honestly, idk what that was. it was just the diner scene from version 3 without the spaceship. we are going to be inside the spaceship, like we're driving it, pull up to the diner. park. the drone is then going to bring us dinner. then we fly off." v4's scale-only push didn't sell piloting anything — just a slow zoom on an otherwise-static scene. Resolved via two follow-up questions, Ben's picks locked as this pass's spec: (1) add a bank/tilt to the arrival, not a rotation-free push and not a continuous idle sway through the whole hold; (2) a NEW quick punch-out departure beat ("then we fly off," not in the original brief), a snappy final kick rather than a full mirrored pull-back. What changed: bankAngle = GAS_BANK_MAX * (1 - camP) — banked GAS_BANK_MAX (-0.06rad) at the very start of the approach, easing to fully level exactly as camP reaches 1 (settle), using the same easeOutCubic curve camP itself already rides, so the level-out lands on the actual parked moment rather than before or after it. Applied via ctx.rotate(bankAngle) between the existing translate-to-focus and scale calls, so rotation and scale share the same pivot (the diner). A new departure punch — departProgress ramps over the final GAS_DEPART_DUR (500ms) of GAS_HOLD, eased with easeOutQuint for a fast-attack "kick" feel (same envelope shape already used for galaxy's hyperspace-snap and harvest's burst-out, reused here rather than inventing a new curve), driving camScale down to GAS_DEPART_MIN_SCALE (0.7) right as the hold ends. Since geShip keeps counting up through bridge3 itself (confirmed by reading tick()'s own geShip = total - T.gasHoldStart — never reset at the phase boundary), the punched-out scale is still in effect while the world's own alpha fades to 0 during the crossfade, reading as "we hit thrusters and the view recedes" rather than a hard cut. Both new motions are force-zeroed under reduced motion: bankAngle multiplies against (1-camP) and camP is already pinned to 1 in the reduced branch (no new reduced-motion code needed there), and departProgress is explicitly short-circuited to 0 when reduced is true. Scope unchanged from v4: diner stop only, no ship sprite, drone beat untouched. Verified via node --check; visual pass and Fable's evidence-check for this iteration follow next.

Step 5 visual pass, iteration 5, 2026-07-22 — one real finding, fixed within the one-attempt rule: ran visual-audit.mjs (34s scoped) and actually compared frames across the arrival/settle/departure window, not just at the touchdown sub-beats. Real bug found: the camera transform (ctx.save()/rotate/scale) had been positioned — since iteration 4, never caught until now — AFTER the ringed planet and both debris-rock mesa layers and the floating island were already drawn, so only the diner building/sign/drone were ever actually inside it. Invisible in iteration 4's mild 0.88→1.0-only push; iteration 5's more dramatic 0.7x departure punch made it obvious by eye: the building visibly shrank away on its own while the island/mesas/planet stayed pinned at full size around it — reading as broken, not as a camera pulling back. Fixed, the one allowed attempt: moved the whole camera-transform setup earlier, right after the background stars, so planet/mesas/island/building/sign/drone all move together as one scene. Re-checked once via a full re-render: confirmed the whole scene now scales and rotates together across arrival, settle, and departure. Retargeted concepts/tools/spot-check.mjs to this file (same 8 sub-beat timestamps as prior iterations) to re-confirm the touchdown-flare/debris co-location fix from iteration 4 still holds — it does. Per the one-attempt rule, no further self-fix looping past this single re-check.

Fable second-opinion pass (evidence-only, per the one-Fable-pass rule), iteration 5, 2026-07-22: confirmed, reading pre-fix and post-fix frames directly: the pre-fix bug is real (island/mesas/planet pixel-identical across arrival/settle/punch frames in the pre-fix bundle, only the sign changing size); post-fix, the whole scene scales together (measured the planet going from partially cropped at settle to fully in-frame and visibly smaller at the punch, island narrowing proportionally); the bank/tilt reads as a genuine whole-scene rotation at arrival-start that levels by settle (flagged as "subtle, partly conflated with the concurrent scale change in a still frame" — a fair read of a deliberately small angle, not a defect); the departure punch holds through the crossfade start, no empty-canvas edges revealed at the pulled-back 0.7x scale (stars correctly stay outside the transform, filling the frame). Touchdown flare/debris co-location from iteration 4 confirmed still intact, though Fable noted the flare reads faint relative to the debris — a polish nit already logged in iteration 4's own notes, not re-litigated further. No unresolved issues. Verdict: ready for Ben. Whether the bank/tilt and punch read strongly enough (vs. too subtly, or too aggressively) at real venue-TV brightness and motion remain explicitly Ben's own review.

v13 (2026-07-22, "-camera" pass) — first finding, before any camera work: v12 never ran. Rendered v12 headless (Playwright + the xdamage stub, same rig as spot-check.mjs) and got ReferenceError: drawFloatingIsland is not defined the moment the diner world's alpha crossed 0.01 — the rAF loop died there, the phase label froze at bridge2 forever, and every diner-stop timestamp captured an unchanging meteor-tail frame. Root cause: v12 deleted the drawFloatingIsland DEFINITION (per Ben's fused-asset direction) but left its CALL in drawGasWorld, and the fused diner+asteroid Recraft image its own comments describe (dinerRockImg) was never actually embedded — the asset sat unwired in .recraft-cache/diner-rock-final.webp while the code still drew the old standalone dinerImg. So the fair baseline for "is the camera landing?" was v11, and v11's frames showed four real problems at the parked 4x zoom, all visible in the captures, none of them the camera path itself: (1) the docked drone rendered as a dark blob LARGER than the diner (sized off screen-space W*0.045 but drawn inside the 4x transform) floating in empty space (its dock point was derived from hand-drawn canopy geometry the Recraft building replaced); (2) the hand-drawn island read as a full-width planet surface at 4x, contradicting "the rock is small"; (3) the diner building floated visibly above the island's surface — literally the complaint ("the diner isn't on the planet at all") v12 was meant to fix; (4) touchdown debris and the 16 clustered pixel-light squares scaled into giant bokeh circles / disembodied blocks. What v13 changed: completed v12's intent — background removed from the cached fused image via Recraft remove_background, embedded as dinerRockImg (alpha-content bounds measured programmatically, encoded in DR_NATIVE), replacing island + standalone diner in one draw call; the drone is now the cached Recraft drone asset (droneImg, bg-removed, downscaled to 360px), sized in WORLD units (~22% of the diner's width) and docked to a measured roof pixel so the v11 size/position class of bug is structurally impossible. Camera: approach now STARTS at GAS_ARRIVAL_MIN_SCALE 0.30 instead of 0.05 — in v11's flight-early frame the destination was an invisible sub-4px speck for ~1.5s (an empty starfield with nothing to push toward); at 0.30 the lit diner is identifiable from frame one beside the planet, establishing the focus point before the push. The easeIn growth (v9's physically-correct call, kept) gets a 560ms easeOut brake tail (approachCurve) — a drive-thru pull-up brakes at the window, it doesn't hard-stop at full speed — and the settle/flare/jolt now key off THIS curve: v9-v12 keyed them off the easeOutCubic camP, which crossed the 0.97 settle threshold ~900ms before the easeIn zoom visually stopped, so the "we've stopped" jolt fired mid-rush. Brake flare + spark puff moved to screen space, low-center (our own thrusters; there is no ground-contact point on a floating rock we never land on). Drone delivery is now a move TOWARD THE CAMERA: launches from the roof in world space, hands off (position- and size-exact via worldToParkedScreen) to a screen-space flight that grows 1.8x as it descends to low-center — dinner brought to OUR window, which the fixed v11 world-point version never sold. Depart punch gains a small opposite-direction bank flick (GAS_DEPART_BANK) so "we fly off" reads as a peel-away turn. Cut: the 16 clustered pixel-light squares (the image carries its own lights; the 8 distant ones stay, screen-space) and the never-called neonBuzz (dead since the sign became an image). Verified by watching the new render, not by intent: full 15-frame pass over the stop plus an 8-frame second pass after three nudges (dock point off the sign board's corner, hover point clear of the rock's bottom tip, delivery blip changed from a solid pink disc — which read as a ball hanging under the drone in the first render — to a soft radial glow). Zero console/page errors across both runs; galaxy (barrel-roll canopy now actually drawn, v12's fix), meteor, and harvest each spot-checked and behaving — none of their code touched by this pass beyond inheriting v12's galaxy call. Honest residuals: (1) all judgments here are from dense still frames, not a continuously-watched play-through — the exact gap that burned v3-v5; the motion is larger and the framing is verifiably right now, but whether it FEELS like piloting at real-time speed is still Ben's watch to call; (2) the fused image's flat-vector style is bolder/more "sticker-like" than the hand-drawn worlds around it — deliberate per Ben's own Recraft direction, flagged not hidden; (3) one v13 render run showed multi-second screenshot latency near the depart beat that did not reproduce on the re-run — believed environmental (capture-side), but if Ben sees late-stop jank on a real TV, profile drawImage of the 1216px webp at 4x before blaming the camera code.

Self-gate
  • Extension, not a parallel system: galaxy/meteor/harvest math is reused verbatim from already-shipped prototypes; only the floating-diner stop and the generalized crossfade are new.
  • Real theme colors only, all four themes, straight from themes/index.js — the floating-diner stop's base palette is still real-theme purple; teal and pink are a deliberate accent addition per Ben's direct creative call (see the 2026-07-22 notes paragraph), not a substitution for the real theme colors.
  • Replay button and reduced-motion toggle both clicked and confirmed working, not just wired.
  • GPU-appropriate: canvas/rAF for all four worlds (genuine per-frame numeric need); letterbox bars are transform-only.
  • No pop-in: the sign flickers on rather than snapping to full brightness; each world crossfades rather than cutting; letterbox bars scale from an edge.
  • Near-black banding guarded: every bridge keeps stars/streaks/pixels active over the crossfade rather than a bare gradient ramp through near-black.
  • Single shared caption element, not one per stop.
  • No dead code: northern lights and firefly meadow were fully removed, not disabled-in-place, when they were cut from the tour.
v14 (2026-07-23) — consolidated from 8 independent Fable candidates, done by hand (no new agents dispatched), not another agent pass: Built on v13-camera.html's diner fix (the most technically complete of 5 independent diner rebuilds — root-caused v12's actual crash: drawFloatingIsland() was deleted but its call site wasn't, so v12 threw a ReferenceError the instant the diner stop faded in and never played for anyone; camera.html's own pass also generated the real Recraft diner+rock, fixed the arrival's dead starting-distance problem, and moved touchdown effects into world-space so they scale with the 4x zoom). The other 4 diner candidates (asset/timing/guest/freshtake lenses) were reviewed but not merged in — they're structurally exclusive alternatives to the camera approach, a creative call for Ben's own review, not a technical merge.

Grafted on top, confirmed byte-identical in the shared sections before touching anything (meteor/harvest code is untouched by any diner-only pass, so this was a safe, verified graft, not a guess):
Meteor shower (from the meteor-buildup candidate) — the two "hero" flyby meteors were flying fully off-screen before ever becoming visible: their old start points (|x0|=0.55/0.6) crossed the projection's z<|x0| singularity at p~0.42-0.47, so a viewer only ever saw a ~3px tick. Fixed with closer start points + an explicit finalZ exit depth so each hero now stays on-screen through a big, bright edge-exit. Trail lag stretched 16ms→110ms (a 1-frame trail read as a dot, not a streak) and both hero durations lengthened so the beat is actually watchable, not a blink. This is what delivers Ben's ask directly: one meteor flies by, a pause, a second one, then the existing swarm ramp picks up.

Harvest/supernova finale (from the supernova-climax candidate, with one real bug fixed in the port): the old burst measured out at mean 74/max 117 of 255 at its own peak frame — literally dimmer than a routine meteor-shower frame (mean 93/max 255), a uniform grey-beige wash, not a climax. Redesigned as contrast rather than uniform brightness: a genuine white-hot additive core (true 255s, localized) plus a shockwave band that now travels the full 660px past the screen corners (the old ring died at H*0.6=324px, never reaching the edges), a pre-burst gather-pip anticipation beat, a cooling afterglow through harSettle, and a small persistent remnant star so the stop ends on "something was born here" instead of an empty ember field. Ejecta count 18→44 with motion-trail streaks (dots don't read as an explosion; moving streaks do), burst stagger halved 180ms→90ms so all the ejecta actually leave within the flash's own peak instead of smearing across two beats. The bug: the candidate this came from had rewritten drawHarWorld's signature to take a new now parameter (needed for the remnant star's twinkle) but never updated its call site — a real, verified break (confirmed by grep, not assumed) that would have thrown at the very first harvest frame. Fixed as part of this port: call site now passes total, and the new state vars (novaCoreR/novaGather/novaAfter/novaRemnant) are reset in resetUI() alongside the pre-existing novaFlash/novaRing so Replay doesn't leak state from a prior play-through — the original pass hadn't reached that step either.

Verification, all done directly (headless Chromium, this file, not trusted from the candidates' own claims): node --check clean on both script blocks. Full real-time render, normal motion: zero page/console errors, all four stops advance correctly, harNova fires exactly inside its 41400-42500ms window. Spot-checked frames by eye: both meteor heroes render as long bright streaks crossing most of the frame width, not ticks; the nova peak frame shows a real white-hot core with a visible orange shockwave ring and 44 distinct ejecta trails radiating outward — reads as an explosion, confirmed by looking, not by the math alone. Correction (see the dated entry below): the "same render under prefers-reduced-motion" claim originally made here was not actually testing reduced motion — see why, next entry.

Not done this pass, flagged not hidden: the other 4 diner-stop candidates' ideas (guest.html's drone-flies-at-camera delivery beat, freshtake.html's windshield-fixed sprite-path structure, timing.html's beat-pacing rework) were not evaluated against or merged with camera.html's approach — that's a real creative decision among 5 working alternatives, not something to default silently. Real-time-feel (not just frame-correctness) for both the meteor beat and the nova climax is, as always in this file, explicitly your own review to make.

v15 (2026-07-23) — Ben's direct call, scrap the destination concept entirely: "we're going to scrap the picking up of the food from a drone. each of the four scenes is just going to be a diff vibe now... no more drone." The diner stop is rebuilt from a single-destination arrival/park/delivery into a movie-style flyby past four separate small business-rocks (diner, motel, arcade, drive-in theater), banking around each in turn, never stopping — "like we're flying by them all as the scene." All four candidates from the prior session (camera/asset/timing/guest/freshtake) are superseded by this rebuild for this stop; none of their single-destination mechanics apply anymore. Deleted outright, not just uncalled: the drone (image, sprite draw, docked/delivery logic, all DRONE_* constants), the single-rock arrival/park/departure state machine (updateGasArrival, approachCurve, SHIP_HOLD_BEFORE/SHIP_FLIGHT_DUR/GAS_BANK_MAX/GAS_DEPART_*/GAS_ARRIVAL_*/GAS_ORBIT_*), the touchdown mechanics (shipLanded/shipThump/shipTouchdownFlare/gasDebrisBits/triggerGasDebris/drawGasDebris/gasIdleGlow) — there is no landing anymore, so nothing to trigger them. The single old dinerRockImg asset is replaced by four freshly Recraft-generated rock+business illustrations (one generation pass, same prompt family, so they read as a consistent set), each background-removed and alpha-content bounds measured programmatically via a headless-canvas scan (not eyeballed — the exact bug class an earlier pass hit anchoring the single diner asset on a guessed center). The new flybyRockFrame() mechanic gives each rock its own approach (closing-distance growth, easeInCubic — the same physically-correct curve this file already validated for the old single-arrival version) and recede, entering from alternating screen edges and continuing through to the opposite side (a real "flew past and kept going," not a there-and-back bounce), with the whole scene banking ±17° toward whichever planet is active — bigger than the old single-arrival bank (12°) since banking IS this stop's whole idea now, not a garnish on a park beat. GAS_HOLD shortened 13000→10600ms (file total now 41,200ms, down from 43,600) since there's no more idle-then-drone-delivery padding to fit — four ~2.5s passes plus lead-in, still within round-journeys.md's 8-14s per-stop range. Reduced motion: a fixed wide shot of all four rocks laid out small in a row, no flight, no bank — verified by actually checking the box, see next.

Also this pass, per Ben's direct call: the midnight-galaxy barrel roll (GAL_ROLL_AT/DUR, drawGalRollCanopy, the roll-progress/visibility computation in tick(), and its call site) is deleted outright — the galaxy stop is back to just the calm drift plus the hyperspace snap, no roll beat.

A real testing-methodology bug found while re-verifying reduced motion for this pass, not a code bug in the stops themselves: this file's reduced flag (line ~138: let reduced = false;) is wired ONLY to the in-page #reducedToggle checkbox's change event — it does NOT read matchMedia('(prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)') at all. Every "verified under prefers-reduced-motion" claim earlier in this file's history (including this same v14 pass's own claim, two paragraphs up) that used Playwright's `reducedMotion` browser-context option instead of actually checking the box was testing normal motion the whole time and calling it reduced — a false-positive verification, the same class of mistake this file's own notes have flagged before for other tools. Re-verified for real this time (page.check('#reducedToggle'), not the context option): the new flyby's reduced branch is confirmed correct by screenshot (all four rocks visible at once, static, no bank); meteor's reduced branch is confirmed genuinely correct too (no z-rush, no hero flybys, faint static twinkle only). But this surfaced a real, pre-existing gap, not caused by today's changes: the harvest stop's ember drift (harAmbient's a.y -= a.speed*dtn in drawHarWorld) and its entire converge→nova→settle sequence (tSinceConverge/novaActive in tick()) have no reduced gating anywhere — confirmed by grep, not assumed. Checking the box during the harvest finale plays the full supernova burst identically to normal motion. This has been true since before today's climax redesign (the pre-climax nova code had the same gap) — it is not something this pass introduced, but it is a real accessibility-contract violation across the file's own stated standard ("reduced motion gets none of it, same contract as every other event in this file"). Deliberately NOT fixed this pass — it's a bigger, separate piece of work than what was asked for today (flyby rebuild + barrel-roll removal), and touching the harvest stop's motion contract wasn't part of that ask. Flagged plainly here so it doesn't get silently lost.

v16 (2026-07-23), same day — Ben, after watching v15: "the flow for the third journey needs to be more flowy. one motion," plus "not a fan of how some of them look" about the four rock illustrations. Two separate fixes:

Flowy rework: the v15 flyby drove each rock through its own independent approach/recede state machine with a genuine 500ms empty-space FLYBY_GAP between passes — technically smooth per rock, but four discrete events stitched together, not one motion. Replaced with a single continuous flightPosition (planet-units) swept linearly across all four rocks over the whole hold; every rock's size/x/bank is the SAME flybyRockFrame() shape function evaluated at that rock's own distance from the current position — one motion generator, not four. First width tried (FLYBY_WINDOW=0.68, just over half the 1-unit rock spacing) was verified WRONG before shipping, not assumed correct: computed the worst-case combined visibility across the whole handoff range and it bottomed out at 16% — small and faint enough, confirmed by actually rendering it, to read as an empty gap despite being technically nonzero. Widened to 1.3 (worst case 68%) so something is always clearly present and moving; re-rendered and confirmed by eye the dip is gone. gasFlyIntensity()'s call sites (both the visual rush layer and the camera-shake amplitude) updated to take the same flightPos instead of raw geShip, so the "are we mid-pass" signal can't drift out of sync with what's actually drawn.

Asset regeneration, per a Fable second-opinion pass dispatched specifically because Ben said he wasn't a fan of some of them (not a vague note — Fable was told to look at the real renders and the raw source images and give a specific, ranked verdict, not just an impression): confirmed 3 of 4 were real, specific failures, not a taste quibble — the arcade's raw image read as "a laptop on a rock" (a blank off-palette white screen, no cabinet silhouette, no controls, holes punched through the rock underneath in-scene); the drive-in was a ticket booth with no screen at all, its one defining feature entirely missing (arcade and drive-in had effectively swapped identities); the motel had zero signage of any kind, reading as an unlabeled train car. Only the diner held up, and Fable's diagnosis was specific and correct: the diner succeeded because it has explicit readable neon text and a balanced building-to-rock ratio, and the other three didn't match that formula despite the file's own prior claim that all four came from "one generation pass, same prompt family." Regenerated arcade, motel, and drive-in against the diner's own proven formula — Fable's own comparison candidate for the arcade (a real cabinet: screen, control panel, "ARCADE" neon marquee) was good enough to ship as-is; motel and drive-in regenerated fresh with the same discipline (motel now has an actual "MOTEL / VACANCY" neon sign and a door row; drive-in now has the big glowing screen as its dominant element plus a "DRIVE-IN" neon booth). All three new images' alpha-content bounds re-measured programmatically (headless-canvas scan, not eyeballed) before being wired into ROCKS' native fields — the diner's own entry is untouched. Verified: node --check clean, full normal-motion render zero errors, reduced-motion re-checked via the actual #reducedToggle checkbox (not the broken Playwright context-option method flagged in the entry above) showing all four rocks laid out static and now all four clearly legible at a glance. Not independently re-verified this pass: whether the new assets read correctly at real venue-TV brightness/distance — same as every prior asset pass in this file, that's explicitly Ben's own call.

v17 (2026-07-23), same day — the second stop rebuilt again, Ben's direct call after a /grilling pass: "not a fan of how some of the [rock illustrations] look... the meteor shower is something maybe we can rework... what do you think about a star wars space battle instead? we're just flying right through it." Grilled and answered explicitly: literal Star Wars (not a generic homage) with the associated trademark/copyright exposure knowingly accepted for this commercial venue; the same continuous-flight-position banking-weave mechanic as the diner flyby, reused rather than re-invented; the existing windshield-crack impact beat kept, re-skinned as battle debris instead of a meteor; 3 ship-vs-ship encounter pairs with the Death Star looming large in the background. X-wing/Y-wing/TIE/Death Star generated via Recraft (Y-wing needed a pure-anatomy prompt with explicit negatives after "starfighter"-adjacent wording kept collapsing to another X-wing; TIE and Death Star regenerated on a white background after the black-background originals turned out to have unfilled regions that would go see-through once background-removed). MS_HOLD/MS_OUT timing, msIntensity's ramp, and the crack/impact mechanic itself are UNCHANGED from the meteor version, verbatim, per the grill decision to keep that beat.

Fable audit pass (independent consultant, no attachment to the build) plus my own follow-up verification against the real code — every finding below confirmed directly, not taken on Fable's word — five real bugs found and fixed: (1) the stop's whole stated idea didn't render — battleEncounterFrame computed a bank value but drawBattleEncounter never read it, so ships flew dead-straight with zero roll despite "banking weave" being the explicit grill decision; now wired into each ship's rot. (2) ships didn't fade with the world during the bridge crossfades — drawBattleShip overwrote globalAlpha with just its own window-alpha instead of composing with whatever was already active; now multiplies against the incoming value. (3) a real teleport/pop-off during the outgoing bridge — tick() calls drawMsWorld with a -99999 sentinel msElapsed once the stop ends, and flightPos/the crack overlay were both recomputing straight off that sentinel, snapping the flight position back to its start (an encounter would re-appear near its entry pose) and cutting the crack off in one frame instead of fading it with the world; both now hold their last real value through the bridge instead of recomputing from the sentinel, reset alongside the file's other replay-state in resetUI(). (4) the crack overlay's fixed screen position landed directly on the Death Star's disc, reading as scaffolding on the sphere rather than a windshield crack; moved to a lower-left position clear of the station. (5) the TIE fighter's near-black hull was barely legible against the plain space background for roughly half its sweep; gave it a static (non-animated, so still GPU-cheap) rim-glow shadow specific to that ship type. Also dropped two pieces of dead code the audit surfaced: peakX in both battleEncounterFrame and the diner flyby's own flybyRockFrame (computed, never read, in both — a pre-existing pattern, not something this build introduced) and battleFlyIntensity (defined, zero call sites, its own comment falsely claiming a tick() integration that never existed).

Also renamed: the on-screen HUD text ("Meteor Shower" / "Departing — Meteor Shower"), the stop-index label, and the page title all still said meteor shower over what's now Star Wars visuals — updated to "Space Battle" throughout. Kept deliberately non-franchise-specific in the visible text itself (the accepted IP risk is the ship/station art, not gratuitous on-screen wording).

Not done, flagged not hidden: Fable also noted the three ship-vs-ship encounters read as visually near-identical (same straight sweep, same envelope, distinguishable mainly by which sprite crosses) and that the exiting/entering encounter groups pile up on the same side of frame at both handoffs — real observations about monotony, not bugs, and not touched this pass; that's a bigger creative rework than the fix list called for. MS_CRACK_SPOKES' geometry wasn't touched, only its anchor point. Re-verified via node --check on both script blocks after every edit in this pass, plus a real headless-Chromium render across the encounters, the bridge2 transition, and the impact/crack beat: zero console/page errors; ships now visibly bank through the weave; the fixed TIE glow reads clearly against black; the crack sits clear of the Death Star; and stepping through the bridge2 frames one at a time confirms the ship/crack teleport-and-pop-off is gone — the exiting encounter now holds its position and fades smoothly with the world into the next stop instead of snapping back. Reduced motion re-checked too, via the actual #reducedToggle checkbox (not the Playwright context-option method this file has already flagged as invalid): still a static wide shot of all three encounters, no bank, no lasers, no shake, TIE glow present — none of this pass's fixes changed that branch's behavior, confirmed rather than assumed.

Barrel-roll re-verification (2026-07-24), independent check, not trusting the v15 entry above at its word: Ben reported the midnight-galaxy barrel roll was still visibly present despite the v15 note above claiming it was "deleted outright." Grepped the live code for every place a rotation could reach the galaxy stop's camera/world — ctx.rotate() calls anywhere in drawGalWorld (none), a rotation term feeding canvas.style.transform in tick() (that line only ever composes translate() from the meteor/flyby shake plus scale() from zoomPunch — no rotate token exists in the string at all), and any surviving reference to GAL_ROLL_AT/drawGalRollCanopy (zero hits, confirmed dead per the v15 entry). Conclusion: the barrel-roll code is genuinely gone — the v15 removal claim holds up under independent re-check, this was not a case of "claimed fixed, actually still there." Most likely explanation for what Ben saw: the hyperspace-snap beat (galSnapEnvelope, ~4200ms into galHold) drives a canvas scale() zoom-punch plus a hard radial star-streak warp and a capped white flash all at once — a fast, disorienting beat that can read as "something spun" at a glance even though it's a pure zoom, not a rotation. Verified directly, not just by reading code: headless-Chromium screenshots at 7 timestamps across the full galHold window (300ms/2000ms/4200ms/4350ms-snap-peak/5200ms/7000ms/9500ms) plus the bridge1 exit — the bright core gradient (fixed at W*0.88,H*0.12) sits in exactly the same screen position in every frame, the letterbox bars stay perfectly horizontal throughout, and the star-warp streaks radiate outward from screen center (a zoom signature) rather than curving tangentially (a roll signature) even at the snap's peak. No code change made here — there was nothing left to remove.

v18 (2026-07-24) — planet-weave rebuild, Ben's direct call: "i want the diner removed from journey 3. id rather have the ship weaving in and out of diff color planets." The four diner/business-rock illustrations (diner/motel/arcade/drive-in) and their asset consts (rockDinerImg/rockMotelImg/rockArcadeImg/rockDriveinImg) are gone, replaced by four freshly Recraft-generated planet spheres — deep-blue ice world, rust-orange desert world, emerald/teal world, violet gas giant — cached as planet-ice/planet-desert/planet-verdant/planet-violet (.webp + .b64 pairs, both present for all four, unlike a few pre-existing cache entries this pass found already missing their .b64 half). Generation needed real iteration, not a one-shot: the model defaulted almost every "planet" prompt to a Saturn-style ring plus a decorative starfield/vignette backdrop regardless of explicit "no rings" wording; what actually worked was reframing the subject as a low-poly faceted "gemstone moon" (not "planet"), an explicit "this is NOT Saturn" line, and naming every unwanted element (ring system, orbiting band, shadow platform, halo, vignette) individually — validated on the ice-world exemplar first, confirmed it background-removed clean with remove_background, then batch-generated the other three off the same locked prompt template with only the color words changed. Two of the four still came back with a stray ring or drop shadow on the first attempt (caught by eye, not assumed correct) and were regenerated once each before being accepted. All four re-rendered through a headless canvas (Playwright, same alpha>10/2px-step scan this file's own history already established) to both measure real alpha-content bounds (not eyeballed) and recompress each asset (canvas.toDataURL('image/webp', 0.82)) — first-generation output ran ~1.1-1.2MB each from the model's own noise/speckle texture, recompressed down to ~140-170KB, in line with this file's existing asset sizes.

The motion itself reuses flybyRockFrame()/flybyWindow() verbatim — one continuous flightPos swept across PLANETS (renamed from ROCKS), same raised-cosine visibility window, same eased scale-up/recede and odd-function bank, same FLYBY_WINDOW/FLYBY_BANK_MAX/GAS_HOLD timing constants — kept at 4 planets (not 5) specifically so none of this file's already-tuned pacing math (FLYBY_END, per-planet timing, the reduced-motion 4-across layout) needed re-deriving. BASE_ROCK_H→BASE_PLANET_H, drawFlybyRock→drawFlybyPlanet, makeRockImg→makePlanetImg, DINER_TEAL→FLYBY_TEAL renamed throughout (all confirmed via grep to be otherwise-unused-elsewhere tokens before renaming); the already-dead DINER_PINK (declared, zero call sites even before this pass) deleted outright rather than carried forward. The existing dominant ringed planet (GAS_PLANET) is kept exactly as-is as the fixed background anchor — it was never diner-specific, and being the only ringed object on screen keeps it from reading as one of the four (deliberately ring-free) weave planets. HUD text ("Pit Stop — The Diner" → "Weaving the Planets"), the stop-name list, the page <title>, and the top-of-page description all updated; the diner-flyby comment references inside the (untouched) space-battle code were also updated for accuracy, since that stop's own comments cited the diner flyby by name as its precedent.

Verified, not assumed: node --check clean on both script blocks. Headless-Chromium, normal motion: zero console/page errors across the full run; screenshots at each planet's peak-visibility timestamp (ice/desert/verdant/violet, computed from FLYBY_START/FLYBY_END/GAS_HOLD) each show a clearly legible, distinctly colored planet against the fixed purple ringed backdrop; a frame captured between two peaks shows the intended continuous handoff (two planets simultaneously partly visible, per FLYBY_WINDOW's overlap) plus a visible whole-scene bank tilt; both bridge crossfades (battle→planets, planets→harvest) checked and cross-fading correctly, including the still-untouched Death Star/X-wing frame from the battle stop it hands off from. Reduced motion checked via the real #reducedToggle checkbox (not the Playwright context-option method this file's own history has already flagged as invalid): all four planets appear at once in a static row, no bank, no motion, checkbox visibly checked in the capture. Self-review finding, not fixed this pass: the violet weave planet and the fixed background GAS_PLANET both land in the same purple/magenta hue family — distinguishable in practice (rings, fixed size/position, and denser facet pattern set the backdrop apart, confirmed by looking at the actual peak-frame capture), but a stricter hue-separation pass (pushing the weave planet further toward magenta, or the backdrop further toward blue-violet) would remove even that ambiguity if it reads as too close on a real venue TV.
